revision.text:
Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival is the most important festival that occurs in [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]] every year. The festival is held for five days from July 19th to 23rd annually. This festival is called [[Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[Meiji period|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 dashi; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]]. <ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.

Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival is the most important festival that occurs in [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]] every year. The festival is held for five days from July 19th to 23rd annually. This festival is called [[Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[Meiji period|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 dashi; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]]. <ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Lots of prototypes are carried from [[Edo period|Edo]] and [[Meiji period|Meij]]<nowiki/>i period; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

There is total of 12 [[dashi(religion)|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Lots of prototypes are carried from [[Edo period|Edo]] and [[Meiji period|Meij]]<nowiki/>i period; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}

Many prototypes are carried from [[Edo period|Edo]] and [[Meiji period]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}
[[Category:Kumagaya]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Gion festivals]]

{{More citations needed|date=December 2017}}
'''Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival''' is a yearly festival that occurs in [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]]. The festival is held for five days from July 19 to 23 annually. This festival is called [[Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[Meiji period|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[Edo period|Edo]] and [[Meiji period]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

'''Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival''' is a yearly festival that occurs in [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]]. The festival is held for five days from July 19 to 23 annually. This festival is called [[Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[Meiji period|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[Edo period|Edo]] and [[Meiji period]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

This festival is called [[Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[Meiji period|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[Edo period|Edo]] and [[Meiji period]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

{{More citations needed|date=December 2017}}
'''Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival''' is a yearly festival that occurs in [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]]. The festival is held for five days from July 19 to 23 annually. This festival is called [[Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[Meiji period|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[Edo period|Edo]] and [[Meiji period]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.

Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. '''Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival''' is a yearly festival that occurs in [[Saitama Prefecture]] [[Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]]. The festival is held for five days from July 19 to 23 annually. This festival is called [[Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[Meiji period|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[Edo period|Edo]] and [[Meiji period]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}

Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[:en:Yakisoba|Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[:en:Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] and [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. Many prototypes are carried from [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] and [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

[[en:Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kumagaya Uchiwa Matsuri}} [[ja:熊谷うちわ祭]]
{{wikidata link|Q11568692}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumagaya Uchiwa Matsuri]][[en:Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival]][[ja:熊谷うちわ祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568692]] Wikidata name: Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More citations needed|date=December 2017}}
'''Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival''' is a yearly festival that occurs in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]]. The festival is held for five days from July 19 to 23 annually. This festival is called [[:en:Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[:en:Yakisoba|Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[:en:Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] and [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[:en:Yakisoba|Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give [[:en:Hand fan|uchiwa]] to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] and [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

'''Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival''' is a yearly festival that occurs in [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]]. The festival is held for five days from July 19 to 23 annually. This festival is called [[:en:Hand fan|Uchiwa]] and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.

More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell [[:en:Yakisoba|Yakisoba]], cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.

Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from [[:en:Edo period|Edo]] and [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

{{wikidata link|Q11568692}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Gion festivals]] [[Category:Kumagaya]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumagaya Uchiwa Matsuri]][[en:Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival]][[ja:熊谷うちわ祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumagaya Uchiwa Matsuri]][[en:Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival]][[ja:熊谷うちわ祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumagaya Uchiwa Matsuri]][[en:Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival]][[ja:熊谷うちわ祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568692]] Wikidata name: Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More citations needed|date=December 2017}}
'''Kumagaya Uchiwa Festival''' is a yearly festival that occurs in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} [[:en:Kumagaya, Saitama|Kumagaya city]]. The festival is held for five days from July 19 to 23 annually. This festival is called {{ill|Hand fan|en|Hand fan|WD=Q193220|lt=Uchiwa}} and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell {{ill|Yakisoba|en|Yakisoba|WD=Q1144102|lt=Yakisoba}}, cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give {{ill|Hand fan|en|Hand fan|WD=Q193220|lt=uchiwa}} to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo}} and {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.

The festival is held for five days from July 19 to 23 annually. This festival is called {{ill|Hand fan|en|Hand fan|WD=Q193220|lt=Uchiwa|1=Hand fan}} and ([[Japanese festivals|matsuri]]), because Uchiwa, the traditional Japanese fans, were distributed to people during the festival in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/|title=関東一の祇園 熊谷うちわ祭|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
There is total of 12 [[Matsuri float|dashi]]; children and adults march through the streets by pulling a long and thick rope attached to dashi. Traffic is controlled during the festival. A 30-centimeter hand gong makes a big sound with musical accompaniment. Each district has dashi and competes for musical accompaniment by putting their dashi next to each other. Kids and adults play ohayashi, Japanese drumming on the [[mikoshi]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/index.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> They practice playing instruments every summer, about two months ahead of the festival.
Uchiwa Festival is one of the biggest summer events in Kumagaya, along with Sakura Festival and Kumagaya fireworks festival. More than 750,000 people participate in the festival, and it is referred to as the number one festival in Kanto region.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/kumagayautiwamaturi/|title=熊谷うちわ祭：熊谷市ホームページ|last=city|first=Kumagaya|website=www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp|language=ja|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref> The streets are lined on both sides with stand-selling shops, which sell {{ill|Yakisoba|en|Yakisoba|WD=Q1144102|lt=Yakisoba|1=Yakisoba}}, cotton candy, Japanese candy, shaved ice, crepes, and many other kinds of foods and toys.
== History == The festival first began in 1750. [[Mikoshi]] was first made in 1830 and around 1902, when merchants started to give {{ill|Hand fan|en|Hand fan|WD=Q193220|lt=uchiwa|1=Hand fan}} to customers. Around the same time, the town grew due to development of the silk industry. Each district began to buy dashi to represent their wealth; they compete which district has bigger and good quality dashi. This was the beginning of the Uchiwa festival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://uchiwamatsuri.com/english/history.html|title=Uchiwa Matsuri official guide|website=uchiwamatsuri.com|access-date=2017-11-22}}</ref>
Many prototypes are carried from {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo|1=Edo period}} and {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}; on March 30, 2012, the festival was designated as an intangible cultural property by Kumagaya city.
